text_blocks.text: early 1960, as a follow-up to Project
Mercury. While the Mercury capsule could
only support one astronaut on a limited
Earth orbital mission, Apollo would carry
ferrying crews to a space station,
lunar landings. The program was named
after the Greek god of light, music, and the
sun by NASA manager Abe Silverstein, who
later said that "I was naming the
spacecraft like I'd name my baby."
Silverstein chose the name at home one
evening, early in 1960, because he felt
"Apollo riding his chariot across the Sun
was appropriate to the grand scale of the
